label: Harassment
scenario_patterns: A person repeatedly sends threatening messages via WhatsApp to an ex-partner
An online troll publishes doxxing information and abusive posts targeting a public figure
A debt collector makes persistent threatening phone calls and visits to a debtor's workplace
A neighbour engages in a sustained campaign of intimidation including verbal abuse and surveillance
legal_tests: Under the Protection from Harassment Act 2014 (POHA), s 3: no person shall use threatening, abusive or insulting words or behaviour causing harassment, alarm or distress

S 4 POHA: no person shall use threatening, abusive or insulting communication likely to cause harassment, alarm or distress (intent not required, but the communication must be heard, seen, or perceived)
S 7 POHA: unlawful stalking — a course of conduct involving acts associated with stalking that causes harassment, alarm or distress
S 10 POHA: doxxing — publishing identifiable information with intent to cause harassment, violence, or fear
For civil remedies, the plaintiff applies for a Protection Order (PO) under s 12 POHA or a court order for false statements under s 15
common_fact_patterns: Cyberbullying and online harassment via social media, messaging apps, or forums
Workplace harassment by colleagues, supervisors, or clients
Stalking by ex-partners involving repeated unwanted contact, surveillance, or following
Doxxing — publishing personal information online to incite harassment against the victim
Neighbour disputes involving persistent verbal abuse, intimidation, or nuisance behaviour
sg_case_references: Benber Dayao Yu v Jacter Singh [2017] SGHC 92 — one of the early POHA protection order applications
Loh Jian Qiang Jason v Tey Kim Hock [2021] SGDC 87 — POHA protection order for stalking and harassment
Tan Eng Hong v AG [2013] SGCA 44 — though a constitutional case, discussed the context of harassment law in Singapore
Public Prosecutor v Ong Wee Teck [2021] SGMC 23 — doxxing under s 10 POHA
Sim Feng Zhi Joel v Loo Wen Xian [2023] SGHC 93 — POHA false statement order and protection order
defences: The conduct was reasonable in the circumstances
The communication was not threatening, abusive, or insulting when assessed objectively
The respondent did not intend and had no reason to believe the conduct would cause harassment (for s 3 POHA)
The publication was fair comment or in the public interest
The alleged stalking did not amount to a course of conduct (isolated incident)
